ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general abalone food product class; no specific braised-abalone-with-mushrooms composite dish class exists. Closest parent match used.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for braised abalone with mushrooms. A0CKP (abalone) used as the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant or home serving of braised abalone with mushrooms is approximately 250 g, comprising 2–3 pieces of abalone with a generous portion of mushrooms and braising sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Abalone, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 174183) and Mushrooms, shiitake, cooked (FDC ID 169243); composite dish values estimated from ingredient proportions with cooking-loss adjustments
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for braised abalone with mushrooms as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for raw abalone (FDC ID 174183) and cooked shiitake mushrooms (FDC ID 169243), weighted by approximate ingredient proportions (abalone ~45%, mushrooms ~25%, sauce/stock ~20%, aromatics ~10%) and adjusted for braising cooking losses and sauce sodium contribution. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce in braising liquid. Values are moderate-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — shellfish and mixed seafood dishes; analogous estimate based on low-GI shellfish and high-protein braised dish profile
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for braised abalone with mushrooms exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on: (1) shellfish and molluscs have negligible direct glycemic impact; (2) the dish's carbohydrate content derives primarily from oyster sauce, soy sauce, and a small amount of cornstarch thickener; (3) high protein content (~10.5 g/100g) and moderate fat content significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (38 × 7.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.84, rounded to 6.8. This is a low GL. Analogous reference: mixed seafood braised dishes with sauce, GI range 35–45 per Atkinson et al. 2008 tables.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100g; ~1050 mg per 250 g serving) from soy sauce and oyster sauce in the braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less, request reduced-sauce preparation, and choose low-sodium soy sauce alternatives to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~38) and moderate carbohydrate content (7.2 g/100g), with high protein attenuating postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised abalone with mushrooms is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 250 g serving; the high protein content and low GI support glycemic control, though the sauce's added sugar and cornstarch should be noted and paired with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load from braising sauce is the primary cardiovascular concern; abalone itself provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, this dish can be consumed occasionally in a 150–200 g portion with reduced sauce; the abalone provides heart-beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fats and selenium, but the high sodium content requires careful monitoring in patients with heart failure or hypertensive heart disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Abalone with Mushrooms (鮑魚燴香菇 / 鲍鱼烩香菇, Bàoyú huì xiānggū) is a prestigious Cantonese Chinese dish featuring whole or sliced abalone slow-braised with shiitake or other premium mushrooms in a rich, glossy brown sauce. The sauce is built from oyster sauce, soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, ginger, garlic, and stock, thickened with cornstarch to a silky consistency. Abalone — a prized marine gastropod mollusc — contributes a firm yet tender, slightly chewy texture with a delicate oceanic umami flavour. Shiitake mushrooms add an earthy, meaty depth and absorb the braising liquid to become intensely flavourful. The dish is a centrepiece of Cantonese banquet cuisine, commonly served at Chinese New Year, wedding banquets, and celebratory family meals. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-calorie dish (approximately 115 kcal/100g) with low saturated fat, meaningful selenium content (22 mcg/100g), vitamin B12 (0.6 mcg/100g), and iron (2.1 mg/100g). The primary clinical concern is sodium (~420 mg/100g) from the braising sauce. The glycemic index is low (~38), making it suitable for glycemic management when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. It is not suitable for strict low-sodium diets without sauce modification.
common_user_queries: How many calories in braised abalone with mushrooms?
Is abalone with mushrooms good for diabetics?
meal_context: Festive lunch or dinner main dish

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for braised abalone with mushrooms as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for raw abalone and cooked shiitake mushrooms, weighted by ingredient proportions and adjusted for braising cooking losses and sauce sodium contribution. The input record contained a significant identity mismatch (name stated 'Braised Abalone with Mushrooms' but description described 'Braised Tofu with Mushrooms'); the abalone identity was adopted as canonical per the food_name field and cr_id. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach and the identity inconsistency in the source record.
notes: IDENTITY CORRECTION: The input record (EX000086) has a critical mismatch — the food_name field states 'Braised Abalone with Mushrooms' and the aliases include abalone-specific Chinese names (鮑魚燴香菇 / 鲍鱼烩香菇), but the description field describes 'Braised Tofu with Mushrooms (红烧豆腐)'. This record has been processed under the canonical name 'Braised Abalone with Mushrooms' as stated in food_name and supported by the abalone-specific aliases. The tofu-related aliases (紅燒豆腐, 红烧豆腐, Hóngshāo dòufu, Hung4 siu1 dau2 fu6) have been excluded from this record as they belong to a different dish. The input description has been replaced with an accurate description of braised abalone with mushrooms. NUTRIENT SOURCE: Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite (abalone FDC 174183 + shiitake FDC 169243); no direct approved-source entry for this composite dish. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, water_g set to estimated values from USDA component data; these carry lower individual confidence. PORTIONS: Input contained four identical duplicate portion entries (250g); deduplicated to one canonical serving. VITAMIN B12: Input listed vitamin_b12 as 0.0; corrected to 0.6 mcg/100g based on USDA abalone data — abalone is a marine animal and contains measurable B12. GI: Estimated at 38 (low) via analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database shellfish/seafood dish profile; no direct measurement available. MULTILINGUAL: This is a Chinese Cantonese dish; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are not applicable and set to null. Chinese simplified and traditional names are present.
